examples.title: Critical Path Method (CPM) Calculation
examples.description: Algorithm to find critical path in a project network
examples.code:
// CPM Algorithm
BEGIN
1. FOR EACH activity in project:
 - Store duration, earliest start (ES), earliest finish (EF)
 - Store latest start (LS), latest finish (LF)

2. FORWARD PASS (Calculate ES and EF):
 - For start activities: ES = 0, EF = duration
 - For other activities: ES = max(EF of all predecessors)
 - EF = ES + duration
 - Project Duration = max(EF of all end activities)

3. BACKWARD PASS (Calculate LS and LF):
 - For end activities: LF = Project Duration, LS = LF - duration
 - For other activities: LF = min(LS of all successors)
 - LS = LF - duration

4. FOR EACH activity:
 - Calculate Float = LS - ES
 - IF Float = 0 THEN activity is on Critical Path

5. OUTPUT: Critical Path sequence and project duration
END
examples.explanation: The algorithm performs forward pass to calculate earliest start/finish times, then backward pass to calculate latest start/finish times. Activities with zero float are critical and directly determine project duration.

examples.title: Three-Point Estimation (PERT)
examples.description: Algorithm to estimate activity duration using optimistic, pessimistic, and most likely estimates
examples.code:
// PERT Three-Point Estimation
BEGIN
INPUT: For each activity i:
 - Optimistic time: t_opt(i)
 - Pessimistic time: t_pess(i)
 - Most likely time: t_likely(i)

FUNCTION CalculateExpectedTime(i):
 // PERT formula
 t_expected = (t_opt + 4*t_likely + t_pess) / 6
 RETURN t_expected

FUNCTION CalculateVariance(i):
 // PERT variance formula
 variance = ((t_pess - t_opt) / 6)^2
 RETURN variance

// Calculate project duration
total_variance = 0
FOR EACH critical path activity i:
 expected_duration = CalculateExpectedTime(i)
 total_variance = total_variance + CalculateVariance(i)
 project_expected = sum of all expected durations

// Standard deviation of project
project_std_dev = SQRT(total_variance)

OUTPUT: Expected project duration with confidence intervals
 - 68% confidence: project_expected ± 1*std_dev
 - 95% confidence: project_expected ± 2*std_dev
 - 99.7% confidence: project_expected ± 3*std_dev
END
examples.explanation: PERT uses beta distribution to calculate expected duration by weighting the most likely time four times more than optimistic and pessimistic times. Standard deviation helps determine confidence levels for project completion.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n = number of activities

examples.title: Earned Value Management Calculations
examples.description: Algorithm to calculate project performance metrics using EVM
examples.code:
// Earned Value Management (EVM)
BEGIN
INPUT:
 - BAC: Budget at Completion (total planned budget)
 - PV: Planned Value (budgeted cost for planned work)
 - EV: Earned Value (budgeted cost for completed work)
 - AC: Actual Cost (actual cost of completed work)

// Schedule Performance
FUNCTION CalculateScheduleVariance():
 SV = EV - PV
 IF SV > 0: Project ahead of schedule
 IF SV = 0: Project on schedule
 IF SV < 0: Project behind schedule

// Cost Performance
FUNCTION CalculateCostVariance():
 CV = EV - AC
 IF CV > 0: Under budget
 IF CV = 0: On budget
 IF CV < 0: Over budget

// Performance Indices
FUNCTION CalculateSPI():
 SPI = EV / PV
 // SPI < 1: behind schedule, SPI > 1: ahead

FUNCTION CalculateCPI():
 CPI = EV / AC
 // CPI < 1: over budget, CPI < 1: under budget

// Estimates at Completion
FUNCTION CalculateEAC():
 // If current variance continues:
 EAC = BAC / CPI
 // OR
 EAC = AC + (BAC - EV) // Original estimate modified

FUNCTION CalculateETC():
 ETC = EAC - AC

FUNCTION CalculateVAC():
 VAC = BAC - EAC

OUTPUT: All calculated metrics
END
examples.explanation: EVM integrates scope, schedule, and cost to provide objective measurement of project performance. SPI and CPI indicate schedule and cost efficiency respectively. EAC projects final project cost based on current performance.
